name: salmon_hatchery_genetic_admixture_wild_stock_risk
premise: State fisheries director Dr. Keiko Matsuda must decide whether to release hatchery-origin Chinook smolts this spring after genetic assays show elevated admixture markers that could dilute endangered wild gene pools. Tribal co-manager Aiyana Begay argues delayed release breaks treaty harvest promises. Biologist Devon Liu models that skipping release collapses commercial quotas already financed on futures contracts. Releasing may trigger ESA consultation that halts logging upstream jointly managed with tribes.

agents.name: Dr. Keiko Matsuda
agents.identity: I am the state fisheries director accountable for ESA consultations, tribal co-stewardship agreements, and commercial quota stability across regions.
agents.goal: I want treaty harvest honored without irreversible genetic swamping of the last wild-origin runs.

agents.name: Aiyana Begay
agents.identity: I am a tribal natural resources co-manager who negotiated release schedules tied to ceremony calendars and winter food security commitments.
agents.goal: I want releases on schedule with stricter spatial sorting even if that costs the state sorting infrastructure we do not yet have.

choices: I will delay release until portable genetic sorting and upper-river penning finish in six weeks, accepting treaty compensation payments and commercial quota haircuts this season.
I will release on schedule with expanded lower-river net fisheries buffers funded by the state, accepting wild admixture risk that ESA partners may later litigate.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Ceremony dates start at new moon and buyers are on the dock. Dr. Keiko Matsuda must choose between delayed sorted release or on-schedule release with funded buffers. What does Dr. Keiko Matsuda do?
